skill.name: global-standards
skill.description: Guides project-wide coding standards, conventions, and best practices across languages and frameworks. Use it to align code style, comments, error handling, validation, technology choices, and repository conventions.
skill.summary: Apply consistent coding standards and project conventions across a codebase.
skill.author: Joseph OBrien

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported 26 potential issues, but manual review found them to be documentation-only false positives. No executable scripts, network access, data exfiltration, prompt injection, or command execution behavior was found in the reviewed files. The skill is safe to publish from this audit.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Standards Words Flagged as Weak Cryptography
low_findings.description: The flagged lines are documentation about descriptive naming, validation, standards, and conventions. They do not reference DES, MD5, SHA1, RC4, or any cryptographic implementation, and no executable code is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review shows prose-only standards guidance at every listed location. There is no code path, algorithm selection, or security control that could use weak cryptography.

low_findings.title: False Positive: General Engineering Guidance Flagged as Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: The flagged lines describe avoiding duplication, avoiding jargon, checking permissions, and explaining comments. They do not instruct system enumeration, host discovery, credential discovery, or data collection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding sections are style, validation, and error-message guidance. No commands, file reads, network calls, or reconnaissance workflow appears in these locations.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Environment File Mentions Are Secret Hygiene Guidance
low_findings.description: The `.env` references advise local development use, templates, and not committing secrets. They do not request reading environment files or exposing secret values.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The content is defensive configuration guidance and includes a warning not to commit secrets. No file access instruction or exfiltration behavior is present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks Around Reference Paths
low_findings.description: The flagged backticks format Markdown file names in a reference list. They are not Ruby or shell backtick execution and do not run commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Each flagged line is a bullet listing a local Markdown reference file. There is no script block, interpreter context, shell invocation, or user-controlled command string.

content.value_statement: Inconsistent code style and conventions slow reviews and make maintenance harder. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code clear standards for code style, comments, errors, validation, and project structure.
actual_capabilities: Applies naming, formatting, and organization standards across common programming languages.
Guides when to add comments, docstrings, TODO notes, and explanatory documentation.
Recommends error handling patterns, logging practices, retry logic, and fallback behavior.
Covers input validation, sanitization, file upload checks, and validation error messages.
Supports technology stack consistency, dependency evaluation, and framework-aligned choices.
Summarizes repository conventions for environment variables, commits, CI/CD, and documentation.
limitations: Does not enforce standards automatically without separate linters, tests, or review tools.
Does not define project-specific rules unless those rules already exist in the repository.
Does not replace security review for production authentication, authorization, or deployment changes.
Provides guidance only; users must decide tradeoffs for their team and codebase.

use_cases.title: Align New Feature Work
use_cases.description: Use the skill before building a feature to match local naming, validation, error handling, and file organization standards.

use_cases.title: Standardize Code Reviews
use_cases.description: Use the skill to check pull requests against consistent standards for comments, structure, dependencies, and maintainability.

use_cases.title: Document Team Conventions
use_cases.description: Use the skill to draft or refine repository standards for commits, CI/CD, environment variables, and project documentation.

prompt_templates.title: Check Basic Code Style
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of a small change
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this change with the global-standards skill. Focus on naming, formatting, comments, and simple maintainability issues.

prompt_templates.title: Review Error Handling
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate review of service or API code
prompt_templates.prompt: Apply the global-standards skill to this API change. Check error types, logging, retry behavior, validation, and user-facing messages.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a New Feature Structure
prompt_templates.prompt: Use global-standards to propose a file structure, naming approach, validation plan, and dependency strategy for this feature.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Repository Conventions
prompt_templates.prompt: Use global-standards to audit this repository for inconsistent conventions across code style, documentation, CI/CD, environment variables, and dependencies.

output_examples.input: Review a pull request that adds a new API endpoint.
output: Identifies naming and file placement issues that differ from nearby endpoints.
Recommends server-side validation and clearer validation error messages.
Suggests narrower exception handling and structured logging with no sensitive data.

output_examples.input: Create standards for a new repository.
output: Provides practical sections for naming, comments, validation, errors, dependencies, and documentation.
Keeps guidance concise so it can become a README or contributing guide.
Notes which rules should be enforced by linting, tests, or CI checks.

output_examples.input: Evaluate whether to add a new dependency.
output: Checks whether existing tools already solve the problem.
Frames maintenance, security, team familiarity, and framework fit as decision criteria.
Recommends documenting the reason for the dependency when it is accepted.

best_practices: Load the most relevant reference file before reviewing a specific change.
Compare recommendations with nearby code before introducing new conventions.
Pair guidance with automated checks when the rule can be enforced reliably.
anti_patterns: Do not use the skill to override established project rules without evidence.
Do not treat style advice as a substitute for tests or security review.
Do not add broad abstractions only to satisfy a generic standard.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps AI assistants apply consistent coding standards, comments, error handling, validation, technology choices, and repository conventions.

faq.question: Does it work with multiple languages?
faq.answer: Yes. The guidance is language-neutral and includes examples for common frontend, backend, and systems code conventions.

faq.question: Can it enforce formatting automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides review and implementation guidance. Use linters, formatters, tests, and CI checks for enforcement.

faq.question: Is it useful during code review?
faq.answer: Yes. It can highlight inconsistent naming, unclear comments, weak validation, poor error handling, and dependency concerns.

faq.question: Does it define security standards?
faq.answer: It includes basic validation and secret-handling guidance, but it does not replace a dedicated security review.

faq.question: How should teams customize it?
faq.answer: Use it with existing repository patterns and team rules. Add project-specific guidance where local conventions are more precise.
